languageFamilies.name: Niger-Congo
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to South Africa
languageFamilies.description: The world's largest language family by number of languages and third-largest by speakers. It spans nearly all of Sub-Saharan Africa and includes the vast Bantu subfamily.
languageFamilies.overview: Niger-Congo is the mother of all Bantu languages, which themselves account for over 500 languages. From the Atlantic coast of Senegal to the southern tip of Africa, Niger-Congo languages unite hundreds of millions of people. The family includes tonal languages, complex noun class systems, and some of the most widely spoken African languages including Swahili, Yoruba, Igbo, and Zulu.

characteristics.name: Noun Class System
characteristics.description: Most Niger-Congo languages use noun classes (similar to grammatical gender but more complex), with prefixes indicating the class of a noun. Bantu languages can have 15-20 noun classes.

characteristics.name: Tonal Languages
characteristics.description: Pitch differences distinguish word meanings. Yoruba has 3 tones, while some languages have up to 5 distinct tones.

characteristics.name: Agglutinative Morphology
characteristics.description: Words are built by adding prefixes and suffixes. A single verb can contain information about tense, subject, object, and mood.

characteristics.name: Serial Verb Constructions
characteristics.description: Multiple verbs string together without conjunctions to describe complex actions, common in Kwa and Voltaic languages.

writingSystem.historical: N'Ko script (invented 1949 for Manding), Vai syllabary (1830s), Nsibidi symbols (Nigeria)
writingSystem.modern: Primarily Latin alphabet with tone marks. Some communities are reviving indigenous scripts.

timeline.event: Proto-Bantu emerges in Cameroon-Nigeria border region

timeline.significance: Completion of one of history's largest migrations

timeline.event: Swahili becomes East African trade language

timeline.significance: First indigenous African script in modern era

timeline.event: N'Ko script invented for Manding languages

subFamilies.name: Bantu
subFamilies.description: Largest subfamily, spanning from Cameroon to South Africa

subFamilies.name: Atlantic
subFamilies.description: West African coastal languages from Senegal to Liberia

subFamilies.name: Mande
subFamilies.description: Languages of ancient Mali and Songhai empires

subFamilies.name: Kwa
subFamilies.description: Coastal West African languages of Ghana, Togo, Benin

subFamilies.name: Volta-Niger
subFamilies.description: Major Nigerian and Beninese languages

subFamilies.name: Gur (Voltaic)
subFamilies.description: Savanna languages of Burkina Faso, Ghana, Togo

distantCousins.relationship: Possible ancient connection debated by linguists
Geographic contact led to click adoption in Nguni languages

uniqueFeatures: Noun classes using prefixes (mu-, ba-, ki-, etc.)
Verb extensions for causative, applicative, passive

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Niger-Congo languages influenced Gullah, Haitian Creole, Brazilian Portuguese, Caribbean English

languageFamilies.name: Afroasiatic
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: North Africa, Horn of Africa, Sahel, Middle East
languageFamilies.description: One of the oldest and most geographically widespread language families, including Arabic, Amharic, Somali, and Hausa. It bridges Africa and the Middle East.
languageFamilies.overview: Afroasiatic languages have shaped world history through the development of writing (Ancient Egyptian, Phoenician → Greek → Latin), monotheistic religions (Hebrew, Arabic), and major civilizations. In Africa, this family includes the Ethiopian Semitic languages with their unique Ge'ez script, the Cushitic languages of the Horn, the Berber languages of the Sahara, and the Chadic languages including Hausa.

characteristics.name: Root-and-Pattern Morphology
characteristics.description: Words are built from consonantal roots (typically 3 consonants) with vowel patterns. Arabic k-t-b → kitab (book), katib (writer), maktaba (library).

characteristics.name: Grammatical Gender
characteristics.description: Two-gender system (masculine/feminine) in most branches, affecting nouns, pronouns, verbs, and adjectives.

characteristics.name: Consonant Inventory
characteristics.description: Rich in pharyngeal and emphatic consonants not found in European languages. Ethiopian languages have ejective sounds.

characteristics.name: VSO/SVO Word Order
characteristics.description: Semitic languages often place verbs first, while others use Subject-Verb-Object like English.

writingSystem.historical: Ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs (3200 BCE), Phoenician alphabet → all Western alphabets, Ge'ez script (Ethiopia)
writingSystem.modern: Arabic script (most widespread), Ge'ez/Ethiopic script (Ethiopia, Eritrea), Latin alphabet (Somali, Hausa), Hebrew script, Tifinagh (Berber)

timeline.event: Proto-Afroasiatic spoken (possibly in Sahara or Horn of Africa)
timeline.significance: Family origin during green Sahara period

timeline.event: Egyptian branch develops along the Nile

timeline.significance: One of world's first writing systems
Ancestor of Greek, Latin, Arabic scripts

timeline.event: Arabic spreads with Islam across North Africa

subFamilies.name: Semitic
subFamilies.description: Includes Arabic, Hebrew, Amharic, Tigrinya - languages of major world religions

subFamilies.name: Cushitic
subFamilies.description: Horn of Africa's indigenous languages - Oromo, Somali, Afar

subFamilies.name: Berber
subFamilies.description: Indigenous North African languages with ancient Tifinagh script

subFamilies.name: Chadic
subFamilies.description: Centered on Hausa, the Sahel's major trade language

subFamilies.name: Egyptian
subFamilies.description: Ancient Egyptian, ancestor of Coptic (now liturgical only)

subFamilies.name: Omotic
subFamilies.description: Ethiopian languages sometimes classified separately

distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Some pronoun and number similarities proposed

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Ethiopian communities worldwide, Arabic cultural sphere, Hausa trade diaspora across Sahel

languageFamilies.name: Nilo-Saharan
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Nile Valley, Sudan, South Sudan, East Africa, Sahara edges
languageFamilies.description: A diverse family of East African languages spoken by pastoralists and farmers from Egypt to Tanzania, including the famous Maasai, Dinka, and Luo peoples.
languageFamilies.overview: Nilo-Saharan languages are spoken by some of Africa's most iconic peoples - the tall Dinka and Nuer of South Sudan, the cattle-herding Maasai, the marathon-running Kalenjin, and the fishing Luo. These are the languages of the Nile Valley's ancient kingdoms and East Africa's cattle cultures. The family includes the Nilotic languages, associated with striking physical characteristics and pastoral traditions.

characteristics.name: Vowel Harmony
characteristics.description: Vowels in a word must be from the same set (ATR harmony). Common in Nilotic languages.

characteristics.name: Vowel Length Distinction
characteristics.description: Short vs. long vowels change word meanings - important in Luo, Dinka, and Maasai.

characteristics.name: Complex Verb Morphology
characteristics.description: Verbs carry extensive information about aspect, mood, direction, and participants.

characteristics.name: Singulative Marking
characteristics.description: Some languages mark singular rather than plural - collective nouns are the default.

writingSystem.historical: Meroitic script (ancient Nubia, partially deciphered), Egyptian influence in Nubian kingdoms
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet with various diacritics, Arabic script for some Nubian languages

timeline.event: Proto-Nilo-Saharan spoken in green Sahara region
timeline.significance: Family emerges during humid African period

timeline.event: Nilotic peoples begin southward migration from Nile Valley
timeline.significance: Cattle culture spreads to East Africa

timeline.event: Nubian kingdoms develop along the Nile

timeline.significance: Pastoralists arrive in Great Rift Valley

timeline.event: Luo migration from South Sudan to Lake Victoria
Kalenjin emerge as distinct political identity

subFamilies.name: Nilotic
subFamilies.description: East African pastoralist languages - Maasai, Luo, Kalenjin, Dinka

subFamilies.name: Eastern Sudanic
subFamilies.description: Ancient Nile Valley languages including Nubian

subFamilies.name: Central Sudanic
subFamilies.description: Languages of South Sudan, CAR, and DRC borders

subFamilies.name: Saharan
subFamilies.description: Desert languages including Kanuri of the Lake Chad region

distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Some shared vocabulary in contact zones

distantCousins.relationship: Ancient Nile Valley contact with Cushitic and Egyptian

uniqueFeatures: Singulative marking (singular, not plural, is marked)
Complex case systems in some branches

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Luo political influence (Obama family heritage), Maasai cultural recognition worldwide

languageFamilies.name: Khoisan
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Southern Africa (Namibia, Botswana, South Africa), isolated groups in Tanzania
languageFamilies.description: Among the world's oldest language families, famous for their click consonants. Spoken by the San (Bushmen) and Khoekhoe peoples - humanity's oldest genetic lineages.
languageFamilies.overview: Khoisan languages are linguistic treasures representing some of humanity's deepest linguistic heritage. The San peoples (formerly called 'Bushmen') have inhabited Southern Africa for over 100,000 years, making them among the oldest continuous populations on Earth. Their click languages influenced neighboring Bantu languages like Zulu and Xhosa, which borrowed click consonants through centuries of contact.

characteristics.name: Click Consonants
characteristics.description: Up to 100+ click consonants in some languages, made by creating suction in the mouth. Written as !, ǂ, ǀ, ǁ, ǃ in phonetic notation.

characteristics.name: Tone Systems
characteristics.description: Complex tonal systems with 4-5 tones that distinguish word meanings.

characteristics.name: Extensive Consonant Inventory
characteristics.description: Some Khoisan languages have over 140 distinct consonants - among the largest inventories known.

characteristics.name: Pharyngeal Sounds
characteristics.description: Various throat sounds beyond clicks, including uvular and pharyngeal consonants.

writingSystem.historical: Oral tradition only - no indigenous writing systems
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet with special symbols for clicks (!, ǂ, ǀ, ǁ, ǃ, ʘ)

timeline.event: Ancestors of Khoisan peoples inhabit Southern Africa
Rock art traditions begin in Southern Africa
Bantu peoples arrive in Southern Africa
timeline.significance: Click sounds borrowed into Nguni languages
Colonial pressure on Khoisan peoples begins

timeline.event: ǃXóõ recognized as most complex sound system

subFamilies.name: Khoe-Kwadi
subFamilies.description: Largest Khoisan group including Nama and Khoekhoe

subFamilies.name: Tuu
subFamilies.description: Southern Africa's original languages, critically endangered

subFamilies.name: Kx'a
subFamilies.description: Northern Kalahari languages

subFamilies.name: Sandawe & Hadza
subFamilies.description: Isolated click languages in Tanzania - possible Khoisan relatives

distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Zulu/Xhosa have 15-20 click sounds from Khoisan

distantCousins.relationship: Some theorize clicks were features of proto-human language

uniqueFeatures: Largest consonant inventories known to linguistics
Complex morphology with many noun classes
Extremely endangered - many have only elderly speakers

empires: No centralized states - egalitarian band societies
historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: San cultural tourism, /Xam folklore preserved in Bleek-Lloyd collection

languageFamilies.name: Bantu
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Central, East, and Southern Africa - from Cameroon to South Africa
languageFamilies.description: The most widespread language subfamily in Africa, result of one of humanity's greatest migrations. From Swahili to Zulu, Bantu languages unite over a third of Africa.
languageFamilies.overview: The Bantu expansion is one of the most significant events in African history. Starting around 3000 BCE from the Cameroon-Nigeria border region, Bantu-speaking farmers spread their languages, iron-working technology, and agricultural practices across central, eastern, and southern Africa over 4,000 years. Today, Bantu languages include Swahili (100M+ speakers), Shona, Zulu, Kikuyu, Lingala, and hundreds more.

characteristics.name: Noun Class Prefixes
characteristics.description: 15-20 noun classes marked by prefixes. Swahili: m-tu (person), wa-tu (people), ki-tu (thing), vi-tu (things).

characteristics.name: Agglutinative Verbs
characteristics.description: Single words express complete sentences: Swahili 'nitakupenda' = ni-ta-ku-penda = I-will-you-love = 'I will love you'

characteristics.name: Vowel Harmony
characteristics.description: Vowels in suffixes harmonize with verb root vowels in many Bantu languages.

characteristics.name: Shared Core Vocabulary
characteristics.description: Common roots across all Bantu: *-ntu (person), *-amba (speak), *-genda (go), *-kúmà (to be heavy)

writingSystem.historical: Primarily oral tradition, though Swahili used Arabic script from ~1100 CE

timeline.event: First wave moves into Congo Basin rainforest
Eastern Bantu reach Great Lakes region
Bantu speakers reach East African coast
Great Zimbabwe civilization built by Shona speakers
Swahili becomes Indian Ocean trade language

subFamilies.name: Nguni
subFamilies.description: Southern African languages with click consonants - Zulu, Xhosa, Ndebele

subFamilies.name: Sotho-Tswana
subFamilies.description: Southern African plateau languages

subFamilies.name: Great Lakes Bantu
subFamilies.description: Languages of Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, and western Kenya/Tanzania

subFamilies.name: Swahili & Coastal Bantu
subFamilies.description: East African coast languages, Swahili is Africa's most spoken Bantu language

subFamilies.name: Congo Basin Bantu
subFamilies.description: Rainforest and savanna languages of DRC, Angola, Congo

uniqueFeatures: Noun class prefix system (mu-/ba-, ki-/vi-, etc.)
Agglutinative verb structure encoding subject, tense, object
Click consonants in southern Nguni languages
Ubuntu philosophy expressed in shared root -ntu

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Swahili taught globally, Zulu cultural exports, Kongo influences in Caribbean

languageFamilies.name: Cushitic
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Horn of Africa - Ethiopia, Somalia, Eritrea, Djibouti, Kenya, Sudan
languageFamilies.description: The indigenous language family of the Horn of Africa, home to Oromo (Africa's 4th most spoken language), Somali, and the ancient Beja.
languageFamilies.overview: Cushitic languages have been spoken in the Horn of Africa for over 7,000 years, making them one of the oldest continuously inhabited language areas. The Oromo are Ethiopia's largest ethnic group, while Somali unites one of Africa's most homogeneous nations. These languages carry rich pastoral traditions, complex clan genealogies, and poetry traditions that rival any in the world.

characteristics.name: SOV Word Order
characteristics.description: Subject-Object-Verb order - 'The man the cow saw' instead of 'The man saw the cow'

characteristics.name: Ejective Consonants
characteristics.description: Consonants pronounced with a burst of air from closed glottis, written as p', t', k', etc.

characteristics.name: Gender and Case System
characteristics.description: Masculine/feminine nouns with complex case marking for subject, object, location, etc.

characteristics.name: Postpositions
characteristics.description: Grammatical markers come after nouns (postpositions), not before (prepositions).

writingSystem.historical: Oral tradition primary; some Arabic script usage for religious texts
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet (Somali since 1972, Oromo since 1991), Ethiopic script (some Oromo)

timeline.event: Proto-Cushitic speakers in Horn of Africa
Islam reaches Somali and Afar coast

timeline.significance: Ethiopia's largest language gets standard script

subFamilies.name: Highland East Cushitic
subFamilies.description: Ethiopian highland languages including Sidama and Hadiyya

subFamilies.name: Lowland East Cushitic
subFamilies.description: Major Cushitic languages - Oromo, Somali, Afar

subFamilies.name: Central Cushitic (Agaw)
subFamilies.description: Ancient Ethiopian languages, possibly original highland inhabitants

subFamilies.name: North Cushitic
subFamilies.description: The ancient Beja language of Sudan and Eritrea

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Somali diaspora worldwide (2M+), Oromo communities in USA/Europe

languageFamilies.name: Semitic
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Middle East, North Africa, Horn of Africa
languageFamilies.description: The ancient language family of Amharic, Tigrinya, Arabic, and Hebrew. In Africa, Ethiopian Semitic languages are home to unique scripts, Orthodox Christianity, and ancient literary traditions.
languageFamilies.overview: Semitic languages in Africa are centered on the Ethiopian highlands, where Amharic, Tigrinya, and Ge'ez have been spoken for over 2,000 years. The Aksumite Empire adopted a unique Ge'ez script that remains in use today - one of the world's oldest continuously used writing systems. Amharic is Ethiopia's national language with 30+ million native speakers, while Tigrinya serves both Ethiopia and Eritrea. These languages carry the legacy of ancient trade routes connecting Africa to Arabia and the Mediterranean.

characteristics.name: Triconsonantal Roots
characteristics.description: Words built from 3-consonant roots with vowel patterns. Amharic s-b-r → säbbärä (broke), t-säbbärä (was broken), mäsbär (breaker).

characteristics.name: Ge'ez Script (Ethiopic)
characteristics.description: An abugida where each character represents a consonant+vowel. 33 base characters with 7 vowel forms each = 231+ characters.

characteristics.name: Ejective Consonants
characteristics.description: Consonants produced with a closed glottis burst (p', t', k', ts', ch'). Distinguishes words like t'ena (health) vs tena (bundle).

characteristics.name: Verb-Final Order
characteristics.description: Unlike Arabic, Ethiopian Semitic languages place verbs at the end of sentences (SOV order).

writingSystem.historical: Ge'ez/Ethiopic script developed ~100 CE from South Arabian, one of Africa's oldest indigenous scripts
writingSystem.modern: Ge'ez script for Amharic, Tigrinya, Ge'ez. Arabic script for Arabic. Hebrew script for Hebrew.

commonPhrases.meaning: May God give you health (formal hello)

timeline.event: South Arabian migrants reach Ethiopian highlands
D'mt kingdom flourishes in northern Ethiopia

timeline.significance: One of Africa's oldest writing traditions

timeline.event: King Ezana converts Aksum to Christianity

timeline.significance: Amharic begins replacing Ge'ez in court

timeline.event: Tigrinya used in Eritrean liberation movement

subFamilies.name: Ethiopic (Ethiopian Semitic)
subFamilies.description: African Semitic languages of Ethiopia and Eritrea

subFamilies.name: Arabic
subFamilies.description: North Africa and Sahel - from Morocco to Sudan

subFamilies.name: Hebrew
subFamilies.description: Ancient language revived in modern Israel

subFamilies.name: South Semitic
subFamilies.description: Southern Arabian and Socotra languages

uniqueFeatures: Triconsonantal root system (k-t-b → write, book, writer, library)
Ge'ez script - 231+ character abugida unique to Ethiopia
Ejective consonants (t', k', p', ch', ts')

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Ethiopian/Eritrean diaspora (2M+), Hebrew language revival, Arabic as global lingua franca

languageFamilies.name: Berber (Amazigh)
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: North Africa - Morocco, Algeria, Libya, Tunisia, Niger, Mali, Mauritania, Egypt
languageFamilies.description: The indigenous language family of North Africa, spoken by the Amazigh (Berber) peoples from the Mediterranean to the Sahara. Carriers of the ancient Tifinagh script and Tuareg desert traditions.
languageFamilies.overview: Berber languages are the original languages of North Africa, predating Arabic by millennia. The Amazigh ('free people') have inhabited the Maghreb and Sahara for over 5,000 years. Today, Berber languages are experiencing a revival with official recognition in Morocco and Algeria. The Tuareg of the Sahara maintain the most vibrant Berber traditions, including the ancient Tifinagh alphabet still used today.

characteristics.name: Consonant Clusters
characteristics.description: Berber allows long strings of consonants: Tmazight 'tfktstt' (you gave it to her). Vowels are minimal.

characteristics.name: Root-Based Morphology
characteristics.description: Like Arabic, words derive from consonantal roots. K-R-Z → akraz (plow), ikraz (field), amekraz (farmer).

characteristics.name: VSO Word Order
characteristics.description: Verb-Subject-Object order, similar to Classical Arabic and Celtic languages.

characteristics.name: Tifinagh Script
characteristics.description: Ancient geometric alphabet descended from Libyan script (3000+ years old). Each consonant is a symbol.

writingSystem.historical: Libyco-Berber inscriptions from 1000 BCE, Tifinagh preserved by Tuareg for 2000+ years
writingSystem.modern: Neo-Tifinagh (official in Morocco), Latin alphabet (common), Arabic script (historical)

timeline.event: Arab conquest brings Arabic and Islam
Almoravid Berber empire from Senegal to Spain

subFamilies.name: Northern Berber
subFamilies.description: Morocco and Algeria's major Berber varieties

subFamilies.name: Tuareg (Tamasheq)
subFamilies.description: Saharan nomadic languages of Niger, Mali, Algeria, Libya

subFamilies.name: Zenati
subFamilies.description: Eastern Berber languages of Algeria, Libya, Tunisia

subFamilies.name: Eastern Berber
subFamilies.description: Isolated Berber communities in Egypt and Libya

uniqueFeatures: Tifinagh - one of Africa's oldest alphabets (3000+ years)
VSO word order with rich agreement systems

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: 1M+ Berbers in Europe (France, Spain, Netherlands), Amazigh cultural revival movements

languageFamilies.name: Nilotic
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: East Africa - Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, South Sudan, Ethiopia
languageFamilies.description: The languages of East Africa's legendary pastoralist peoples - Maasai, Dinka, Luo, Kalenjin, and Turkana. Speakers are renowned for cattle culture, athletic prowess, and warrior traditions.
languageFamilies.overview: Nilotic peoples migrated from the Nile Valley southward over thousands of years, bringing cattle-centered cultures to East Africa. The Maasai became iconic symbols of African tradition, the Kalenjin dominate world distance running, and the Luo produced global leaders like Barack Obama's father. These languages share cattle terminology, age-set systems, and pastoralist traditions that define East African culture.

characteristics.name: ATR Vowel Harmony
characteristics.description: Advanced Tongue Root harmony - vowels in a word must share the same tongue position (tense or lax).

characteristics.name: Vowel Length Distinction
characteristics.description: Short and long vowels change meaning. Dholuo: 'bur' (hole) vs 'buur' (mole). Critical for understanding.

characteristics.name: Elaborate Cattle Vocabulary
characteristics.description: Hundreds of terms for cattle colors, horn shapes, ages, and conditions. Cattle are central to culture.

characteristics.name: Tonal Systems
characteristics.description: Pitch distinguishes words and grammar. Luo and Dinka have complex tone patterns.

writingSystem.historical: Oral tradition - elaborate oral poetry, praise songs, and genealogies
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet with tone marks where used

timeline.event: Proto-Nilotic peoples in Nile Valley region

timeline.significance: Cattle culture spreads to East Africa

timeline.event: Luo migration from South Sudan to Lake Victoria
Kalenjin age-sets organize resistance to colonial expansion
Kalenjin identity consolidates in independent Kenya
Kalenjin runners begin dominating world athletics
Barack Obama (Luo heritage) elected US President

subFamilies.name: Western Nilotic
subFamilies.description: Luo, Dinka, Nuer, Shilluk - peoples of Lake Victoria and South Sudan

subFamilies.name: Eastern Nilotic
subFamilies.description: Maasai, Turkana, Samburu - East Africa's iconic pastoralists

subFamilies.name: Southern Nilotic
subFamilies.description: Kalenjin group and Datooga - Kenya and Tanzania highlands

uniqueFeatures: Elaborate cattle terminology (100+ terms for cattle)
Tall stature - Dinka and Maasai among world's tallest peoples

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Obama family (Luo), Kenyan runners worldwide, Maasai cultural tourism

languageFamilies.name: Songhai
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Niger River Valley - Mali, Niger, Burkina Faso, Benin, Nigeria
languageFamilies.description: The language of one of Africa's greatest empires - the Songhai Empire that dominated West Africa in the 15th-16th centuries. Today spoken along the Niger River from Timbuktu to Nigeria.
languageFamilies.overview: Songhai languages are the linguistic legacy of the mighty Songhai Empire, which at its height controlled the trans-Saharan trade routes and made Timbuktu one of the world's great centers of learning. The Songhai people built upon the earlier Mali Empire's foundations and created an even larger realm. Today, Songhai languages remain important in the Niger River valley, with Zarma (Djerma) being a major language in Niger's capital Niamey and surrounding areas.

characteristics.name: SOV Word Order
characteristics.description: Subject-Object-Verb word order, placing the verb at the end of sentences - different from many African languages.

characteristics.name: Postpositions
characteristics.description: Uses postpositions (after nouns) rather than prepositions, following the head-final pattern.

characteristics.name: Tonal System
characteristics.description: Two-tone system distinguishing high and low tones to differentiate words and grammatical functions.

characteristics.name: Noun Suffixes
characteristics.description: Grammatical relationships marked by suffixes on nouns, including definite articles and plurals.

writingSystem.historical: Arabic script used during Songhai Empire era for scholarly and religious texts in Timbuktu
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet with special characters for sounds not in European languages; some Arabic script use continues

timeline.event: Proto-Songhai speakers settle along the middle Niger River
Songhai establish the city-state of Gao
Sunni Ali begins Songhai Empire expansion
Askia Muhammad takes power, golden age begins
timeline.significance: Peak of Songhai learning and trade

timeline.event: Moroccan army defeats Songhai at Tondibi

subFamilies.name: Northern Songhai
subFamilies.description: Languages of the Saharan trade routes including Tadaksahak

subFamilies.name: Southern Songhai
subFamilies.description: Major languages of the Niger River valley including Zarma

distantCousins.relationship: Songhai is often classified within Nilo-Saharan but some linguists consider it an isolate
distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Some vocabulary similarities with Saharan languages

distantCousins.relationship: Centuries of contact with Mande-speaking neighbors
Heavy Islamic influence during empire era
distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Many Arabic loanwords for religion, governance, trade

uniqueFeatures: SOV (Subject-Object-Verb) word order - rare in Africa
Complex verbal morphology with many suffixes
Strong connection to Islamic scholarly tradition (Timbuktu manuscripts)

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Songhai trade diaspora across Sahel, Timbuktu's legacy as center of African Islamic learning, manuscripts preserved worldwide

languageFamilies.name: Mande
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: West Africa - Mali, Guinea, Senegal, Gambia, Sierra Leone, Liberia, Ivory Coast
languageFamilies.description: The language family of the great Mali Empire and its legendary emperor Mansa Musa. Mande languages spread across West Africa through trade, migration, and the influence of medieval African empires.
languageFamilies.overview: Mande languages are spoken by the descendants of one of Africa's most influential civilizations. The Mali Empire (1235-1600) spread Mande languages, Islam, and trade networks across West Africa. Mande peoples developed the N'Ko script in 1949, one of Africa's most successful modern writing systems. The griot tradition of oral historians and musicians remains central to Mande culture today.

characteristics.name: SOV Word Order
characteristics.description: Subject-Object-Verb word order - the verb comes at the end of sentences, unlike most Niger-Congo languages.

characteristics.name: Tone Systems
characteristics.description: Two or three tones distinguish words. Tonal melodies also carry grammatical information.

characteristics.name: Noun-Adjective Order
characteristics.description: Adjectives typically follow nouns, as in 'house big' rather than 'big house'.

characteristics.name: Postpositions
characteristics.description: Uses postpositions after nouns rather than prepositions before them.

writingSystem.historical: Arabic script (Ajami) used for religious and administrative texts since the Mali Empire
writingSystem.modern: N'Ko script (invented 1949 by Solomana Kante), Latin alphabet widely used, Arabic continues in Islamic contexts

timeline.event: Proto-Mande speakers establish along the Niger River
Sundiata Keita founds the Mali Empire
Mansa Musa's famous pilgrimage to Mecca
Mali Empire declines, successor states emerge
Mande traders establish networks across West Africa

subFamilies.name: Western Mande
subFamilies.description: Major languages of the Mali Empire heartland

subFamilies.name: Eastern Mande
subFamilies.description: Languages of the eastern Mande region

subFamilies.name: Southern Mande
subFamilies.description: Forest region languages of Liberia and Ivory Coast

distantCousins.relationship: Mande is a major branch of Niger-Congo, though its exact position is debated
distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Some shared vocabulary with other Niger-Congo branches

distantCousins.relationship: Centuries of geographic contact in Senegambia

distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Many Arabic loanwords for religion, learning, trade

uniqueFeatures: N'Ko script - successful modern African writing system
Griot tradition (jeli) - hereditary oral historians and musicians
Strong association with trans-Saharan gold trade

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Mandinka influence in Gambia/Senegal, Dyula trade diaspora across Sahel, Mande music influences global music (Ali Farka Touré, Salif Keita)

languageFamilies.name: Chadic
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Nigeria, Niger, Chad, Cameroon, Central African Republic
languageFamilies.description: The largest branch of Afroasiatic in Africa by number of languages, dominated by Hausa - one of Africa's most widely spoken languages with 80+ million speakers and a major trade lingua franca across the Sahel.
languageFamilies.overview: Chadic languages are centered around Lake Chad and the Sahel region. Hausa, the family's giant, has been a trade language for centuries, spreading across West Africa through the famous Hausa merchant networks. The family includes nearly 200 languages, making it the most linguistically diverse branch of Afroasiatic. Hausa's influence extended along trans-Saharan trade routes, and today it's one of Africa's major languages with rich literary traditions.

characteristics.name: Tone Systems
characteristics.description: Most Chadic languages are tonal, with 2-3 tones distinguishing word meanings and grammatical functions.

characteristics.name: Grammatical Gender
characteristics.description: Two-gender system (masculine/feminine) inherited from Afroasiatic, affecting nouns, pronouns, and verb agreement.

characteristics.name: Aspect-Prominent Verbs
characteristics.description: Verbs focus on whether actions are completed or ongoing (aspect) rather than when they happen (tense).

characteristics.name: Lateral Fricatives
characteristics.description: Distinctive sounds not found in most world languages, including lateral fricatives and implosives.

writingSystem.historical: Ajami script (Arabic) used for Hausa literature since the Sokoto Caliphate (1804)
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet (Boko) is standard; Arabic script (Ajami) continues for religious and some literary texts

timeline.event: Proto-Chadic speakers settle around Lake Chad
Islam reaches Hausaland through trans-Saharan trade
Hausa becomes major trade language across Sahel

timeline.significance: Hausa becomes one of Nigeria's national languages

subFamilies.name: West Chadic
subFamilies.description: Dominated by Hausa, the giant of Chadic languages

subFamilies.name: Central Chadic (Biu-Mandara)
subFamilies.description: Diverse languages of the Mandara Mountains region

subFamilies.name: East Chadic
subFamilies.description: Languages around Lake Chad and eastern regions

subFamilies.name: Masa
subFamilies.description: Languages of the Logone River region

distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Some grammatical gender patterns, pronoun structures

uniqueFeatures: Hausa is Africa's 3rd most spoken indigenous language
Rich oral and written literary tradition (Hausa)
Extensive trade vocabulary from centuries of commerce

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Hausa spoken in Ghana, Togo, Cameroon, Niger; Hausa traders established communities across West Africa; BBC Hausa and VOA Hausa reach millions

languageFamilies.name: Omotic
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Southwestern Ethiopia, along the Omo River valley
languageFamilies.description: The indigenous languages of Ethiopia's Omo Valley - one of humanity's most linguistically and culturally diverse regions, home to ancient traditions and unique cultural practices.
languageFamilies.overview: Omotic languages are spoken by some of Africa's most culturally distinctive peoples in Ethiopia's Omo River valley. This UNESCO World Heritage region is home to tribes with unique body painting traditions, lip plates, and agricultural practices. Omotic is sometimes classified as a separate family from Afroasiatic, highlighting its distinctiveness. Languages like Wolaytta, Gamo, and Kafa represent ancient Ethiopian heritage predating Semitic and Cushitic migrations.

characteristics.name: SOV Word Order
characteristics.description: Subject-Object-Verb order, placing the verb at sentence end - typical of Ethiopian area languages.

characteristics.name: Case Marking
characteristics.description: Nouns marked for grammatical case (nominative, accusative, genitive) through suffixes.

characteristics.name: Verb-Final Structure
characteristics.description: Complex verbs with multiple suffixes for tense, aspect, mood, and person.

characteristics.name: Ejective Consonants
characteristics.description: Glottalized stop consonants produced with airstream compression - shared with Ethiopian Semitic.

writingSystem.historical: Primarily oral tradition; some early contact with Ge'ez script
writingSystem.modern: Latin-based alphabets developed for each major language; Ge'ez script occasionally used

timeline.event: Omotic cultures flourish in Omo River valley
UNESCO recognizes Omo Valley cultural significance
Lower Omo Valley declared UNESCO World Heritage Site

subFamilies.name: North Omotic
subFamilies.description: Major languages including Wolaytta and Gamo

subFamilies.name: South Omotic (Aroid)
subFamilies.description: Languages of the lower Omo Valley, including Hamar and Aari

subFamilies.name: Mao
subFamilies.description: Endangered languages of the Omo-Gibe region

distantCousins.relationship: Geographically adjacent; some scholars see them as related within Afroasiatic
Millennia of contact and language influence

uniqueFeatures: Coffee originates from Kafa region (coffee named after Kafa)
Unique lip plate and body painting traditions
Some of Africa's most endangered languages
Rich oral tradition with historical epics

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Wolaytta diaspora in Addis Ababa and abroad, coffee culture's global spread from Kafa origins

languageFamilies.name: Atlantic
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: West African coast - Senegal, Gambia, Guinea-Bissau, Guinea, Sierra Leone, Mauritania
languageFamilies.description: The indigenous languages of the Atlantic coast of West Africa, including Wolof (Senegal's dominant language) and Fula/Fulfulde (spoken by the widespread Fulani people across the Sahel).
languageFamilies.overview: Atlantic languages stretch along West Africa's Atlantic coast and deep into the Sahel through the Fulani pastoral migrations. Wolof dominates Senegalese culture and is the language of Dakar's vibrant urban scene. Fula (Fulfulde/Pulaar) is spoken by 40+ million Fulani herders from Senegal to Sudan. The Serer people of Senegal maintain some of Africa's most sophisticated traditional astronomical and agricultural knowledge in their language.

characteristics.name: Noun Class Systems
characteristics.description: Complex noun class systems using prefixes and suffixes, with 15-20 classes in some languages marking animate/inanimate, human/animal, etc.

characteristics.name: Consonant Mutation
characteristics.description: Initial consonants of words change based on grammatical context - distinctive feature of Fula and other Atlantic languages.

characteristics.name: Verbal Extensions
characteristics.description: Verbs modified with suffixes for causative, reciprocal, reflexive, and other meanings.

characteristics.name: Complex Morphology
characteristics.description: Words built through extensive prefixing and suffixing, especially in noun phrases.

writingSystem.historical: Arabic script (Wolofal for Wolof, Ajami for Fula) used since Islamic contact
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet standard; Arabic script maintains some use for religious texts

timeline.event: Proto-Atlantic speakers along West African coast
Serer establish agricultural civilization in Senegambia
Fulani jihads spread Islam and Fula influence

timeline.significance: Wolof becomes de facto national language

subFamilies.name: Northern Atlantic (Senegambian)
subFamilies.description: Wolof, Serer, and related languages of Senegal and Gambia

subFamilies.name: Fula-Serer
subFamilies.description: Fula/Fulfulde and closely related Serer languages

subFamilies.name: Bak Languages
subFamilies.description: Diola, Manjak, and related languages of Guinea-Bissau region

subFamilies.name: Mel Languages
subFamilies.description: Temne, Kissi, and coastal Guinea/Sierra Leone languages

distantCousins.relationship: Atlantic is a major branch of Niger-Congo
Both branches of Atlantic-Congo within Niger-Congo

uniqueFeatures: Fula spoken across 18+ countries by pastoralists

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Large Wolof/Senegalese diaspora in France and USA, Fulani communities across West Africa, Atlantic Creoles in Cape Verde and Guinea-Bissau

languageFamilies.name: Kwa
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: West African coast - Ghana, Togo, Benin, southeastern Ivory Coast
languageFamilies.description: The languages of the Gold Coast and Slave Coast - Akan (Ashanti, Twi, Fante), Ewe, Fon, and Ga. These languages powered the Ashanti Empire and the Dahomey Kingdom.
languageFamilies.overview: Kwa languages are spoken along the Gulf of Guinea coast, in regions historically known as the Gold Coast and Slave Coast. Akan (including Twi and Fante) is the dominant language of Ghana, spoken by the Ashanti, Fante, and other Akan peoples who built powerful kingdoms. Ewe and Fon are the languages of Vodun (Voodoo) religious traditions that spread to the Americas. The Ga people of Accra speak a Kwa language distinct from their Akan neighbors.

characteristics.name: Tonal Systems
characteristics.description: Complex tone systems with 2-4 tones, where pitch distinguishes word meanings and grammatical functions.

characteristics.name: Serial Verb Constructions
characteristics.description: Multiple verbs strung together without conjunctions to describe complex actions - a distinctive Kwa feature.

characteristics.name: Subject-Verb-Object Order
characteristics.description: Basic SVO word order similar to English, with topic-comment structures.

characteristics.name: Noun Class Remnants
characteristics.description: Simplified noun class systems compared to Bantu, with fewer distinctions.

writingSystem.historical: Oral tradition; some early literacy through European contact
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet with various diacritics for tones; Adinkra symbols (Akan) for cultural concepts

timeline.event: Proto-Kwa speakers in West African forests
Ashanti Empire founded by Osei Tutu I
Ghana becomes first sub-Saharan country to gain independence
Ghanaian highlife and hiplife gain global fame

subFamilies.name: Akan (Tano)
subFamilies.description: Ghana's dominant language group including Twi, Fante, and Baule

subFamilies.name: Gbe (Ewe-Fon)
subFamilies.description: Languages of Vodun traditions - Ewe, Fon, and related languages

subFamilies.name: Ga-Dangme
subFamilies.description: Languages of Accra and the eastern Gold Coast

subFamilies.name: Nyo (Potou-Tano)
subFamilies.description: Southwestern Ghana and southeastern Ivory Coast languages

distantCousins.relationship: Sister branch within Niger-Congo, including Yoruba and Igbo

uniqueFeatures: Serial verb constructions ('He took it went gave me')

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Akan day-names in Jamaica and Americas, Vodun/Voodoo traditions in Haiti and Brazil, Ghanaian diaspora in UK and USA

languageFamilies.name: Gur
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Sahel and savanna - Burkina Faso, Ghana, Mali, Togo, Benin, Ivory Coast, Niger
languageFamilies.description: The languages of the savanna and Sahel - Mossi (Burkina Faso's majority), Dagbani, and Gurma peoples. Speakers of these languages built the powerful Mossi kingdoms that resisted both Mali and Songhai empires.
languageFamilies.overview: Gur (also called Voltaic) languages are spoken across the West African savanna, centered on Burkina Faso. The Mossi people, speaking Mooré, form Burkina Faso's largest ethnic group and maintained independent kingdoms for centuries. The Mossi states uniquely resisted both Islamization and conquest by the Mali and Songhai empires. Gur languages extend into northern Ghana (Dagbani, Mampruli) and across the Sahel to Niger and Benin.

characteristics.name: Noun Class Systems
characteristics.description: Extensive noun class systems with suffixes marking singular/plural and semantic categories (human, animal, object).

characteristics.name: Tonal Systems
characteristics.description: Two or three tones distinguish words and grammatical categories.

characteristics.name: SVO Word Order
characteristics.description: Basic Subject-Verb-Object order with topic-prominent sentence structures.

characteristics.name: Reduplication
characteristics.description: Word parts repeated for plurals, intensification, or repeated action.

writingSystem.historical: Primarily oral tradition; limited Arabic script use in Islamic areas
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet with special characters for sounds unique to Gur languages

timeline.event: Proto-Gur speakers in West African savanna
Dagomba and Mamprusi kingdoms at height
timeline.significance: Gur kingdoms in northern Ghana flourish

subFamilies.name: Oti-Volta (Central Gur)
subFamilies.description: Mossi, Dagbani, Gurma and related languages

subFamilies.name: Grusi
subFamilies.description: Languages of Ghana-Burkina border region

subFamilies.name: Lobi-Dagara
subFamilies.description: Languages of southwestern Burkina Faso and northern Ghana

subFamilies.name: Senufo
subFamilies.description: Languages of the Senufo people of Ivory Coast, Mali, Burkina Faso

distantCousins.relationship: Sister branch within Niger-Congo, share border in Ghana
Centuries of contact in Sahel region

uniqueFeatures: Mossi kingdoms resisted Mali and Songhai empires
Thomas Sankara was Mossi - 'Africa's Che Guevara'

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Mossi labor migration to Ivory Coast and Ghana, Burkinabè diaspora in France, Dagomba influence in northern Ghana politics
